evaluationBriefText:
IBM
Vendor Evaluation Brief
FIS TMS Managed Services RFP | March 30, 2026 | Confidential
1. Vendor Files Identified
IBM_FIS_TMS_Managed_Services_RFP_Response_IBM_Mar_26.pdf (36 pages)
IBM_Appendix_B__TMS_RFP__Vendor_Response_Workbook_IBM_26Mar2026.xlsx (9 tabs)
IBM_Appendix_C_-_Draft_FIS_SOW_IBM_March_26.docx (SOW redlines)
2. Evaluation Tear Sheet
Total data points: 39
3. Completeness Summary
Total data points: 39
4. Executive Assessment Memo
IBM
Bottom Line
IBM delivers the lowest TCV in the field ($460M) with the best Year 1 performance ($76.9M savings vs baseline) — but this headline number is structurally misleading. The Y1 "savings" are an artifact of phased take-on pricing (Q1 at just $2.2M rising to $31M by Q4) that defers cost rather than reducing it; Y2 peaks at $122.8M making it the second-most-expensive Y2 in the field. COLA is set at 0% for all countries with a note that it "will be mutually agreed" — meaning the quoted $460M TCV will increase materially once COLA is negotiated. Tab 9.0 (efficiency assumptions) is entirely blank, the pricing is explicitly described as not constituting "a commitment or offer," and the proposal reads as a consulting framework rather than a binding operational bid. IBM brings unmatched AI and technology credentials (#1 AI market share, 100K+ agents migrated) but the commercial maturity of this proposal is the weakest in the field.
Underlying Assumptions in their Proposal
Pricing structure: Phased ramp-up model where Q1Y1 is $2.2M (minimal scope) growing to $31M by Q4Y1, then peaking at $33M in Q1Y2. This is not a Day-1 take-on — it’s a gradual assumption of scope. FIS retains operational responsibility (and cost) for the portion not yet transitioned. (Tab 6.0 C119–J119)
COLA: Explicitly 0% for ALL countries with the comment: "COLA has not been included in our current price and will be mutually agreed." This means the $460M TCV is a floor, not a ceiling. If COLA is negotiated at 3% for developed markets and 5% for offshore (industry standard), the true TCV rises to ~$510–530M. (Tab 6.0 C41–C46)
Efficiency assumptions: Tab 9.0 is COMPLETELY BLANK. IBM redirects to Tab 7.4 but does not provide the standard offshore:onshore mix, labor arbitrage %, AI/Digital %, productivity %, or certainty commitment. This makes it impossible to validate the savings mechanism. (Tab 9.0 C8–G12)
Non-binding: The proposal explicitly states it "does not constitute a commitment or offer by IBM. A more detailed study...is needed before a formal price can be released." This is the only vendor that explicitly disclaims binding intent. (Proposal p.8)
Strengths & Differentiators
Strongest tech governance posture tied with Genpact: 11 of 12 Commit (incl. open APIs, 12-month coexistence, AI governance, quantified projections) and 1 Partial (tech debt). This is the joint-best governance score. (Tab 5.0 C33–C44)
Unmatched AI/technology credentials: #1 AI market share for 5 consecutive years, 50K+ AI engagements, watsonx platform, IBM Consulting Advantage. No vendor brings this depth of enterprise AI capability. If FIS views this engagement as an AI transformation play, IBM is the technology leader. (Proposal p.6)
Most conservative pricing with no lock-in: No minimum volume, no minimum contract duration, no upfront investment conditions. IBM’s proposal is the most flexibility-friendly in the field, giving FIS maximum optionality to scale up/down or exit. (Tab 6.0 C81–C85)
100K+ contact center agents migrated: IBM has the largest claimed migration track record of any vendor. (Proposal p.6)
Key Risks & Concerns
This is not a binding commercial offer: IBM explicitly disclaims commitment on pricing, calls it a "preliminary estimate," and conditions the $11.3M investment on full scope award. No other vendor uses this language. FIS cannot compare this to binding offers from Cognizant, Genpact, or EXL. (Proposal p.8; Tab 6.0 D81)
Tab 9.0 is entirely blank: IBM is the only vendor that did not complete the efficiency assumptions tab. Without offshore:onshore mix, labor arbitrage, AI/digital overlay, productivity gains, and certainty commitment, FIS has no mechanism to validate how $290.6M in savings are generated. (Tab 9.0)
Y1 savings are illusory: The $76.9M "savings" in Y1 occur because IBM is billing only $67.3M while FIS’s baseline is $144.2M. But IBM is only operating a fraction of the scope in Y1 (Q1=$2.2M). FIS is still paying for the rest. The real test is Y2 when IBM is at full scope — and Y2 savings drop to $24.3M, missing the FIS target. (Tab 6.0 C119–J119)
COLA at 0% is a deferred cost: Once COLA is added (3–5% annually), the $460M TCV increases by $50–70M over 5 years. This erases IBM’s apparent cost advantage vs. Cognizant ($499.9M COLA-inclusive) and EXL ($477M). (Tab 6.0 C41–C46)
Key Questions to Pressure-Test in Workshop
Your proposal states it "does not constitute a commitment or offer." Under what conditions will IBM convert this to a binding proposal with fixed pricing? What is the timeline?
Tab 9.0 is blank. Provide the offshore:onshore mix, labor arbitrage %, AI/digital %, productivity %, and certainty % for each geography. What contractual certainty will IBM commit to?
The Y1 cost of $67.3M implies IBM operates only partial scope in Y1. What % of FIS volume/FTE does IBM assume it is operating in each quarter of Y1? What does FIS retain?
If COLA is added at 3% for developed markets and 5% for offshore, what is the revised 5-year TCV? Provide the COLA-adjusted pro-forma.
Your AI tools (Cobee Pro, Klarity, Procedure Eater, ICA) appear to be consulting/productivity tools for IBM’s own teams, not operational AI overlays for contact center agents. Which tools directly reduce FTE in the contact center, and by how much?
Go-Forward Implications
IBM’s non-binding posture creates a structural negotiating opportunity: FIS can use IBM’s $460M as a market reference point to pressure-test binding offers from other vendors, even if IBM itself does not advance to the shortlist.
The phased ramp-up model (Q1=$2.2M) is a fundamentally different commercial construct that FIS should evaluate as an alternative to Day-1 rebadge models. If FIS prefers gradual transition, IBM’s approach may be the right template — but it needs to be priced as binding.
IBM’s AI tools appear more suited to IBM’s own consulting/delivery teams than to contact center agent productivity. FIS should distinguish between "vendor enablement" AI and "operational" AI that directly reduces FTE/AHT.

snippets.questionText: Describe your approach to managing sub-contractors used in delivering TMS services. Confirm: (1) whether sub-contractors would be used on this engagement, which subcontractors would be used and where work would be performed; (2) your SLA flow-down mechanism; (3) whether the client retains audit rights over sub-contractors.
snippets.text:
Approach to Managing Sub‑Contractors
IBM’s delivery preference for FIS Total Managed Services (TMS) is to perform services using IBM employees and IBM‑owned delivery assets, limiting sub‑contracting to narrowly defined, non‑core components where it demonstrably adds value and is explicitly approved by FIS. IBM’s model is intentionally designed to minimize subcontractor dependency during transition and steady-state operations, reducing operational and regulatory risk for first-time outsourcing programs. All subcontractors will operate under IBM security, compliance, and operational standards, including PCI-DSS, GDPR, and banking regulatory requirements. IBM remains the prime contractor and single point of accountability for all services, outcomes, SLAs, and compliance obligations.

1. Use of Sub‑Contractors, Identity, and Delivery Locations
Based on our current understanding of the FIS TMS scope, IBM does not anticipate the routine use of sub‑contractors for core managed services, including:
- Contact center operations (voice, chat, messaging)
- Back‑office processing (disputes, chargebacks, fraud support, account maintenance)
- Workforce management, quality, training, reporting, and operations management
These services would be delivered by IBM resources from IBM’s global delivery network (onshore, nearshore, and offshore locations aligned to client, regulatory, and language constraints).
Sub‑contractors may be proposed only where appropriate and subject to prior FIS approval, typically limited to:
- Specialized technology components (e.g., niche AI utilities, language processing, document capture tools) that integrate into IBM‑managed platforms
- Country‑specific or regulated services where local licensing or legal requirements necessitate a locally registered provider
- Cloud or infrastructure services (e.g., hyperscalers or regulated hosting providers) supporting IBM‑managed solutions
Where sub‑contractors are proposed:
- They will be pre‑vetted through IBM’s Third‑Party Risk Management framework
- Fully disclosed in the SOW, including name, scope of services, delivery locations, and data access
- Bound to IBM’s standards for data protection, information security, regulatory compliance, ethics, and resiliency
IBM remains fully responsible for delivery, regardless of sub‑contractor involvement.

2. SLA Flow‑Down Mechanism
IBM applies a single, integrated SLA and performance management framework across IBM teams and any approved sub‑contractors.
Key elements include:
- Contractual flow‑downAll applicable client obligations—SLAs, KPIs, security, confidentiality, audit, and regulatory requirements—are explicitly flowed down into sub‑contractor agreements. From FIS’s perspective, performance and protections are equivalent to IBM‑only delivery.
- Aligned performance metricsFIS TMS SLAs (e.g., ASA, AHT, FCR, backlog aging, dispute turnaround, quality and compliance metrics) are decomposed into supplier‑level KPIs where relevant. Sub‑contractor performance is measured as part of the end‑to‑end service, not in isolation.
- Continuous monitoring and escalationSub‑contractor performance is monitored through IBM’s service management tooling and governance processes. Performance shortfalls trigger corrective action plans, escalation, and—if required—replacement at IBM’s expense, consistent with contractual provisions.
- Integrated governanceSub‑contractor performance, risks, and remediation actions are reviewed within the same operational, management, and executive governance forums used for the overall FIS TMS engagement.
IBM remains fully accountable for meeting all contractual SLAs irrespective of any sub‑contractor participation.

3. Client Audit Rights Over Sub‑Contractors
Yes. IBM’s standard model ensures that FIS retains appropriate audit rights over any sub‑contractors involved in delivering TMS services.
Specifically:
- Sub‑contractor agreements include provisions permitting IBM—and FIS where required—to audit relevant processes, controls, and records related to the services.
- Sub‑contractors are required to provide independent assurance reports (e.g., SOC 2 Type II or equivalent) where applicable and to remediate identified issues.
- IBM will provide FIS with audit summaries, assurance reports (as permitted), and remediation status.
- Where FIS requires direct audit access, IBM will structure the arrangement to enable such rights while maintaining confidentiality and security protocols.

snippets.questionText: Optional. Are there any industry-relevant certifications, training frameworks, or accreditations held by your workforce that are applicable to financial services contact center operations?
snippets.text:
Industry‑Relevant Certifications, Training Frameworks, and Accreditations
Yes. IBM maintains a comprehensive set of corporate certifications, workforce training frameworks, and role‑based accreditations specifically applicable to financial services contact center and back‑office operations. These credentials are embedded into delivery governance and are mandatory for practitioners supporting regulated financial services activities.

1. Enterprise Certifications and Control Frameworks
IBM operates financial services contact center and back‑office services within a certified and independently attested control environment, including:
- ISO 27001 – Information Security Management
IBM maintains an ISO 27001–certified Information Security Management System (ISMS), providing structured controls for the protection of customer data, PII, authentication credentials, and financial information across all delivery locations and channels.
- ISO 9001 – Quality Management
IBM’s ISO 9001–certified quality framework governs process design, training, quality assurance, error management, and continuous improvement for contact center and back‑office operations, supporting regulatory accuracy and consistency.
- SOC 1 / SOC 2 / SOC 3
IBM maintains independently audited SOC reports covering controls related to security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, and privacy. These reports are commonly used by financial services clients for vendor risk and audit purposes.
- PCI DSS
For services involving payment card data, IBM operates PCI DSS–aligned delivery environments, including segmented cardholder data environments, restricted access controls, secure agent desktops, and continuous monitoring.
- Global Data Privacy Compliance
IBM aligns with applicable global and regional privacy regulations (e.g., GDPR and equivalent regimes), including lawful processing, data minimization, retention controls, and cross‑border data transfer safeguards required for multinational financial services programs.

2. Agent‑Level Certifications and Mandatory Training
All practitioners supporting financial services contact center operations are subject to role‑based certification and ongoing compliance training, including:
- Regulatory and Compliance Training
Mandatory onboarding and recurring training covering:
    - AML and KYC awareness
    - Fraud awareness and escalation procedures
    - Fair treatment of customers and conduct risk
    - Complaints handling and regulatory timeframes
    - Sensitive data handling and privacy obligations
- Role‑Based Account Certification
Agents must complete and pass formal certification prior to handling live customer interactions. Certification includes:
    - Identity verification and authentication protocols
    - Required scripts, disclosures, and consent language
    - Product‑specific servicing requirements (e.g., cards, deposits, loans)
    - Escalation and exception handling aligned to regulatory standards
- Continuous Learning and Re‑Certification
Knowledge refreshers, assessments, and targeted retraining are triggered by regulation changes, process updates, or QA findings to ensure sustained compliance.

3. Quality, Analytics, and Leadership Credentials
IBM’s support and leadership workforce includes professionals with recognized quality and operational excellence credentials:
- Lean / Six Sigma (Green and Black Belt) applied to contact center optimization, error reduction, and compliance‑critical workflows such as disputes and investigations
- Project and Delivery Management Training aligned to PMI and IBM internal standards
- Dedicated Quality Assurance and Compliance Leads trained in monitoring regulated interactions and resolving root causes
Quality teams leverage a combination of human QA and AI‑enabled speech/text analytics to identify compliance risks, missed disclosures, and training gaps across 100% of interactions.

4. AI‑Enabled Training and Compliance Enablement
IBM augments traditional training with AI‑enabled learning and compliance support, including:
- Simulation‑based learning using real‑world financial services scenarios (e.g., fraud indicators, hardship cases, regulatory complaints)
- AI‑assisted prompts and guidance to support compliant language and next‑best actions during live interactions
- Continuous feedback loops linking QA findings directly to personalized coaching and retraining plans

snippets.questionText: What were your voluntary agent attrition rates for each of the last three calendar years? Break out by geography where materially different.
snippets.text:
Voluntary Agent Attrition – Last Three Calendar Years
IBM Consulting’s customer operations and contact center services maintain voluntary attrition rates that are materially below typical BPO industry benchmarks, particularly for regulated and financial‑services programs. This performance is supported by our AI‑enabled operating model, structured career paths, and proactive workforce management.

While IBM does not disclose client‑ or site‑specific attrition figures, the data below reflects representative voluntary attrition ranges across our global Customer Care and Customer Operations portfolio for the last three calendar years, with geographic differentiation where material.

Voluntary Attrition – Global (Frontline Agents)
- Global average (last 3 years): ~18 – 22% per annum
- Attrition has remained relatively stable year‑over‑year, with modest improvement in mature, AI‑enabled programs where automation and self‑service reduce agent workload and burnout.

Voluntary Attrition by Geography (Indicative Ranges)
North America (United States, Canada)
- ~15 – 20%
- Generally lower attrition on regulated and complex programs, including financial services, where roles emphasize problem resolution, judgment, and customer advocacy rather than high‑volume transactional handling.

Nearshore Americas (Latin America)
(e.g., Mexico, Costa Rica, Colombia, Argentina, Brazil)
- ~18 – 22%
- Attrition varies by local labor markets and wage dynamics but remains below regional BPO averages due to bilingual career paths, cross‑training, and AI‑supported operations.

Europe (Western, Central & Eastern Europe)
- ~12 – 18%
- Typically the lowest attrition globally, particularly in multilingual and regulated environments (e.g., financial services, healthcare), where tenure and specialist skill depth are higher.

Asia‑Pacific (India, Philippines, other APAC hubs)
- ~20 – 24%
- These are structurally more competitive labor markets; however, IBM’s attrition remains favorable relative to local benchmarks, with stabilized financial‑services programs trending toward the lower end of this range.

Middle East & Africa / Smaller Delivery Hubs
- ~15 – 22%
- Attrition varies by language mix and market maturity but is generally consistent with nearshore performance.

Context and Stabilization
- Total attrition (voluntary + involuntary) across large‑scale customer operations typically falls in the ~22–26% range annually.
- Mature, long‑running programs—especially in financial services—frequently operate below 20% total attrition once stabilized.

Key Drivers of Attrition Performance
Attrition outcomes are driven by several structural elements embedded in IBM’s delivery model:
- AI‑enabled workload reduction: Automation, virtual agents, and digital workflows handle a significant share of routine interactions, reducing cognitive load and burnout for human agents.
- Self‑service and containment: Mature programs often achieve 40 – 60%+ digital containment, shifting agents toward more complex and meaningful work.
- Proactive workforce monitoring: Workforce signals (schedule adherence, quality trends, workload spikes) are monitored continuously to enable early intervention.
- Clear career progression: Defined advancement paths (agent → senior agent → SME → team lead / analyst) support longer tenure across geographies.
- Stabilization curve: On new programs or post‑transition, attrition initially reflects inherited workforce dynamics; within 12–18 months, programs typically converge to the ranges shown above as the operating model matures.

Summary
Over the last three calendar years, IBM’s voluntary agent attrition has averaged ~18–22% globally, with Europe at the low end, North America and nearshore centers in the mid‑range, and major APAC hubs higher but still competitive versus market benchmarks. These levels support continuity, productivity, and service stability for large‑scale, regulated engagements such as FIS TMS.

snippets.questionText: Describe your organization's overall track record serving card-issuing financial institutions. Include years in market, number of active issuer clients, approximate ACV managed, and any notable performance milestones.
snippets.text:
Overall Track Record Serving Card‑Issuing Financial Institutions
IBM Consulting has an established, global track record supporting card‑issuing financial institutions across credit, debit, prepaid, and co‑brand portfolios, as well as adjacent issuer servicing and regulated banking operations.

Years in market
IBM has served financial institutions for 50+ years and has operated formalized managed services and BPO for financial services for over 20 years, including issuer operations, customer care, disputes and chargebacks, collections, and regulated back‑office functions. Card issuer operations are a long‑standing component of IBM’s Financial Services Operations and Business Transformation portfolio, delivered across North America, EMEA, LATAM, and APAC.

Number of active issuer clients
IBM currently supports dozens of financial services clients with in‑scope card‑issuing or issuer‑adjacent operations, including Tier 1 global banks, regional issuers, digital banks/fintechs, and commercial and consumer co‑brand programs. Across these engagements, IBM supports tens of millions of active card accounts, delivered through both blended and dedicated models. Individual client names and exact sub‑segment counts are not disclosed due to confidentiality obligations.

Approximate ACV managed
Within IBM's 2025 Corporate Revenue total of $67.5B, IBM Consulting Revenue was $21.1B, this includes the BPO, advisory and AI-led transformations. IBM’s financial services operations manage multi‑billion‑dollar ACV globally. Issuer‑related managed services programs commonly range from $10M to $50M+ ACV per issuer, reflecting multi‑year, multi‑tower engagements spanning customer care, back‑office operations, and technology‑enabled transformation.

Notable performance milestones
Across card‑issuing and regulated financial services engagements, IBM has delivered:
- 30 – 40% reductions in cost‑to‑serve through AI‑enabled automation, self‑service expansion, and optimized global delivery models.
- First Contact Resolution levels of 75–80% and call abandonment rates below 3% through omnichannel transformation and agent‑assist AI.
- ≥99.6% service availability for critical issuer workflows, supported by resilient operations and enterprise‑grade controls.
- Up to 80 – 85% automation of routine back‑office activities, including dispute intake, chargeback processing, and standard card maintenance tasks.

Industry recognition  
Our standing as a partner for financial and issuer operations is reinforced by independent recognition, including:
- Leader in 2025 Gartner® Magic Quadrant™ for Finance & Accounting BPO
- Leader in 2024 Gartner® Magic Quadrant™ for API Management
- HFS Top 10 rankings for Finance and Accounting Outsourcing and Source‑to‑Pay  
- 2024 Everest Group Leader,  Finance & Accounting Outsourcing  (FAO) PEAK Matrix® Assessment 
- Hackett Digital Awards, 2024 Digital Award, Plan-to-Results
These acknowledgements reflect IBM’s ability to deliver scaled, AI‑enabled, compliant operations for large, regulated financial institutions, including card issuers.

Controls, compliance, and governance
Issuer services are delivered within a mature control environment, including PCI DSS‑aligned delivery where cardholder data is in scope, ISO 9001 quality controls, and ISO 27001/SOC 2 security and data‑protection frameworks. IBM operates an enterprise AI Control Centre that provides real‑time visibility into volumes, SLAs, automation performance, and compliance metrics, supported by structured operational governance and executive oversight.

Summary
IBM brings decades of issuer‑services experience, global delivery scale, and proven AI‑enabled operating models to reliably manage large card‑issuing portfolios, significant ACV, and high‑stakes service levels for regulated financial institutions.

snippets.questionText: What is your total headcount across all geographies? Break out by: (1) front-office agent FTE; (2) back-office agent FTE; (3) support FTE (QA, WFM, Training, Management, Analytics); (4) approximate onshore / nearshore / offshore split (%).
snippets.text:
Total Global Headcount and Workforce Mix
IBM Consulting operates a large, global customer operations and managed services workforce supporting contact center and back‑office services across all geographies. The figures below reflect IBM’s Customer Operations / Customer Transformation BPO population and are provided as indicative ranges appropriate for an engagement of this nature. IBM can flex this mix based on FIS requirements, including increasing onshore or EU-based capacity to meet regulatory and client expectations.

Total Headcount (Global)
- Approximate total BPO headcount: 22,000 FTE+
    - Distributed across 25+ dedicated BPO delivery centers and 30+ service and innovation centers worldwide
    - Supporting client programs across regulated and non‑regulated industries, including financial services

Headcount Breakdown by Role
1) Front‑Office Agent FTE
(Voice, chat, messaging, secured digital channels; Tier 1 / Tier 1.5 support)
- Approximate share: 55 – 65% of customer operations workforce
- Indicative range: 12,000 – 14,000 FTE globally
Front‑office agents are organized into journey‑ or product‑aligned squads and are augmented by AI‑assisted tools (agent assist, intelligent routing, self‑service deflection). Routine inquiries are increasingly handled by digital workers, with human agents focused on complex, regulated, or empathetic interactions.

2) Back‑Office Agent FTE
(Case management, disputes, chargebacks, account maintenance, exception handling, processing)
- Approximate share: 20 – 30%
- Indicative range: 4,500 – 6,500 FTE globally
Back‑office teams handle judgment‑based and regulated tasks and work alongside automation and digital workers that execute data gathering, validation, reconciliation, and workflow routing to improve cycle times and accuracy.

3) Support FTE
(QA, WFM, training, operations management, analytics, AI ops, SMEs)
- Approximate share: 10–15%
- Indicative range: 2,000–3,000 FTE globally
Support roles include quality assurance, workforce planning, coaching and training, analytics and insights, delivery management, and dedicated AI operations and digital‑worker supervision roles that ensure performance, compliance, and continuous optimization.

Onshore / Nearshore / Offshore Split (Human FTE)
IBM’s delivery model is intentionally global, balancing regulatory, language, cost, and resiliency requirements.
- Onshore: ~25–30%
    - United States, Canada, Western Europe, Japan, Australia
    - Focus on regulated work, client‑specific constraints, senior escalation handling, and leadership roles
- Nearshore: ~25–30%
    - Latin America, Eastern Europe, select EMEA hubs
    - Multilingual coverage, regional alignment, and cost‑efficient delivery for voice and digital channels
- Offshore: ~40–50%
    - India, Philippines, and select Asia‑Pacific locations
    - Primary scale engines for omnichannel Tier 1 support, back‑office processing, and AI/automation operations

Digital Workforce Context
In parallel with human FTE, IBM deploys a significant digital workforce (virtual agents, automated workflows, agentic AI) that handles or accelerates 60–80% of eligible contacts and transactions in mature programs. These are governed through IBM’s enterprise AI Control Centre and are not counted as human FTE, but materially reduce cost‑to‑serve and scale requirements.

Summary
IBM operates a global customer operations workforce of approximately 22,000 FTE, with a balanced mix of front‑office, back‑office, and support roles and an approximately 25–30% onshore / 25–30% nearshore / 40–50% offshore distribution. This human workforce is complemented by a large, governed digital workforce, enabling scalable, compliant, and cost‑efficient delivery for large, regulated managed services programs such as FIS TMS.

snippets.text:
Supported Languages and Associated Delivery Locations
IBM Consulting operates a scaled, enterprise‑grade multilingual delivery network, supporting 40+ languages globally across contact center and back‑office services through a combination of native‑language agents, multilingual teams, and AI‑enabled digital workers. IBM’s language delivery model is aligned to regulated financial services requirements, including PCI and regional data residency considerations. Below is a focused view of the languages of particular relevance to FIS TMS and the primary delivery locations supporting each.

Languages of Particular Relevance:

English (US, Canada, UK variants)
- United States: Multiple IBM delivery centers supporting US English for regulated and customer‑facing programs
- Canada: Toronto and other bilingual hubs (English‑CA)
- United Kingdom: London and regional UK delivery centers (English‑UK)
- India: Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Chennai, Pune, Gurgaon (large‑scale English for voice, chat, and back‑office)
- Philippines: Manila, Naga (English‑heavy, voice‑intensive and empathetic support)
- Poland, Romania, Czech Republic: Pan‑European English support for blended queues
English delivery is supported across onshore, nearshore, and offshore locations and augmented by AI‑assisted agent support and self‑service.

Spanish (US and LATAM variants)
- United States: Bilingual English/Spanish teams for US Hispanic consumers
- Mexico: Monterrey, Guadalajara
- Costa Rica: San José
- Colombia: Bogotá
- Argentina: Buenos Aires
These locations support US Spanish and regional Spanish variants for customer service, disputes, and back‑office processing.

French‑Canadian (and European French)
- Canada: Bilingual delivery centers supporting French‑Canadian and English
- France: Paris (European French)
- Belgium: French/Dutch bilingual teams
- Romania, Poland: EU multilingual hubs supporting French for blended and written work
French‑Canadian delivery is handled by native or near‑native speakers with language‑specific quality assurance.

German
- Germany: Native German delivery centers
- Poland, Czech Republic, Hungary: Multilingual hubs supporting German and English
- Romania: German for back‑office and written interactions
Digital self‑service and AI‑assist tools are configured for German‑language terminology and regulatory messaging.

Dutch
- Netherlands: Dutch language support
- Belgium: Dutch/Flemish bilingual teams
- Poland, Romania: Multilingual EU hubs supporting Dutch for customer and back‑office services

Portuguese
- Brazil: São Paulo, Hortolândia (Brazilian Portuguese – primary hub)
- Portugal: Braga (European Portuguese, as required)
- Colombia / Costa Rica: Regional multilingual pods supporting Portuguese alongside Spanish and English

Broader Multilingual Coverage
Beyond the languages listed above, IBM supports a wide range of additional languages through its global delivery network, including:
- European languages: Italian, Polish, Czech, Hungarian, Spanish (EU), Greek, Nordic languages
- Asia‑Pacific languages: Mandarin, Cantonese, Japanese, Korean, Hindi, Tagalog, Malay
- Middle East & Africa: Arabic, French/English combinations
Lower‑volume or written interactions can be supported through structured workflows and AI‑enabled translation under controlled quality and compliance standards.

Summary
IBM supports the full set of languages required for FIS TMS—including English (US/CA/UK), Spanish (US), French‑Canadian, German, Dutch, and Portuguese—through a diversified global delivery network. Language services are delivered primarily from IBM‑owned centers and are augmented by AI‑enabled self‑service and agent‑assist capabilities to ensure scalable, high‑quality, and compliant multilingual support.

snippets.questionText: What is your average agent tenure overall? Break out by primary function where available: Customer Service, Fraud Operations, Disputes & Chargebacks, Debt & Collections, Back Office, and Risk & Compliance.
snippets.text:
Average Agent Tenure by Function
Across IBM Consulting’s Customer Operations and Managed Services portfolio, we maintain strong and stable tenure profiles, particularly in regulated financial‑services environments. IBM’s tenure profile supports continuity and knowledge retention critical for regulated financial services operations such as those within FIS. Our operating model—combining experienced human talent with AI‑enabled automation and structured career progression—supports retention above industry averages for comparable scopes.

The figures below reflect typical average tenure observed across large‑scale customer service and back‑office programs similar in complexity to FIS TMS. Actual tenure varies by geography and program maturity, but these ranges are representative of steady‑state delivery.

Average Agent Tenure (Overall)
- Overall average tenure: ~3.0 – 3.5 years
- Mature programs (steady‑state): Senior agents, specialists, and SMEs commonly reach 4.0–5.0+ years

Tenure by Primary Function
Customer Service (Customer Care, Servicing, Advocacy)
- Frontline agents: 2.5 – 3.0 years
- Senior agents / SMEs / team leads: 3.5 – 5.0+ years
Customer service roles benefit from AI‑assisted tools and self‑service deflection that reduce repetitive work and support higher job satisfaction and longer tenure.

Fraud Operations
- Fraud analysts: 3.0 – 3.5 years
- Senior fraud specialists / investigators: 4.0 – 5.0+ years
Fraud roles typically demonstrate higher tenure due to specialized training, regulatory skill depth, and clear progression paths into senior analyst and QA roles.

Disputes & Chargebacks
- Disputes analysts: 3.0 – 3.5 years
- Senior dispute / chargeback specialists: 4.0 – 5.0+ years
These roles require scheme‑rule expertise and judgment‑based decisioning, supporting longer tenure relative to general customer service functions.

Debt & Collections
- Early‑stage / financial care agents: 2.5 – 3.0 years
- Senior collectors / hardship specialists: 3.5 – 4.5+ years
Tenure is supported through analytics‑driven segmentation, empathetic servicing models, and automation that handles routine outreach and account actions.

Back Office (Processing, Case Management, Operations Support)
- Processors / case handlers: 3.0 – 3.5 years
- Senior processors / SMEs: 4.0 – 5.0+ years
Back‑office teams typically show strong stability, with clear career paths into QA, analytics, and supervisory roles.

Risk & Compliance (AML, KYC, Conduct, Regulatory Operations)
- Analysts: 3.5 – 4.0+ years
- Senior analysts / compliance specialists / QA: 4.5 – 6.0+ years
Risk and compliance functions exhibit some of IBM’s longest tenure, reflecting the depth of domain knowledge required and strong professional development pathways.

Stability Drivers
Across all functions, tenure is sustained through:
- Automation and AI‑enabled workflows that reduce repetitive tasks and burnout
- Structured role progression (agent → senior agent → SME → lead / analyst)
- Continuous training and certification aligned to regulated environments
- Proactive workforce management and performance monitoring
On new programs, tenure naturally reflects the inherited workforce profile during transition. Within 12–18 months of stabilization, programs typically converge to the ranges above and continue to improve as automation and process maturity increase.

Summary
IBM’s average agent tenure across customer service and back‑office operations is ~3.0–3.5 years overall, with higher tenure in regulated, judgment‑based functions such as fraud, disputes, and risk & compliance. This stability supports service continuity, SLA reliability, and effective knowledge retention for large‑scale, regulated managed services programs like FIS TMS.

snippets.questionText: In which countries do you currently deliver contact center or back-office services? Please list all the countries and sites you currently support. For each, indicate whether the site is company-owned or sub-contracted.
snippets.text:
Countries and Sites Used for Contact Center and Back‑Office Services
IBM Consulting delivers contact center and back‑office services through a large, diversified global delivery network. IBM’s delivery model prioritizes IBM-owned centers for consistency, security, and regulatory compliance for financial services clients. The overwhelming majority of delivery is performed from IBM‑owned and IBM‑operated facilities, governed under IBM’s global standards for security, data protection, regulatory compliance, and quality. Sub‑contracted facilities are used only on an exception basis for niche language coverage, country‑specific requirements, or temporary surge capacity, and always under IBM contractual control and oversight.

Below is a consolidated view of current delivery countries and representative sites, with ownership noted.

North America – Onshore
Ownership: IBM‑owned
- United States: Boulder, Dallas, Denver, Raleigh, Atlanta, Tulsa, Austin, Greenville, Winston‑Salem
- Canada: Toronto, Calgary
These sites primarily support regulated, complex, and client‑facing work, including financial services contact center and sensitive back‑office operations.

Latin America – Nearshore
Ownership: IBM‑owned
- Mexico: Monterrey, Guadalajara
- Costa Rica: San José
- Colombia: Bogotá
- Brazil: São Paulo, Hortolândia
- Argentina: Buenos Aires
These centers deliver multilingual contact center and back‑office services (Spanish, Portuguese, English), supporting both regional and global programs.

Europe – Onshore and Nearshore
Ownership: IBM‑owned
- United Kingdom: London
- Netherlands: Amsterdam
- France: Paris
- Spain: Madrid
- Portugal: Braga
- Poland: Kraków
- Czech Republic: Prague
- Hungary: Budapest
- Germany: Multiple IBM delivery locations
- Romania: IBM delivery centers
These sites support EMEA contact center and regulated back‑office services, including multilingual and country‑specific operations.

Asia‑Pacific – Offshore and Regional
Ownership: IBM‑owned
- India: Bengaluru, Chennai, Hyderabad, Pune, Mumbai, Gurgaon, Delhi / Noida
- Philippines: Manila, Naga
- China: Dalian
- Japan: Tokyo, Sapporo, Hiroshima
- South Korea: Seoul
- Malaysia: Kuala Lumpur
- Australia: Ballarat, Brisbane
India and the Philippines are IBM’s largest hubs for omnichannel contact center and back‑office delivery, including automation‑enabled and AI‑assisted operations.

Africa & Middle East
Ownership:
- Egypt: Cairo (IBM‑owned / operated)
- South Africa: IBM‑operated facilities
For specific multilingual or regional requirements, IBM may supplement capacity with approved subcontracted partners in select locations (e.g., North Africa). Any such use is disclosed, contractually controlled, and governed under IBM security, compliance, and quality standards.

Use of Sub‑Contracted Sites
- Sub‑contracted delivery is limited and non‑core.
- All subcontracted sites are:
    - Pre‑approved through IBM’s third‑party risk framework
    - Bound by the same security, confidentiality, compliance, and SLA obligations as IBM‑owned sites
    - Fully integrated into IBM’s service management and governance model
IBM remains the prime contractor and single point of accountability for all services, regardless of delivery location or subcontractor involvement.

Summary
IBM currently delivers contact center and back‑office services across North America, Latin America, Europe, Asia‑Pacific, Africa, and the Middle East, primarily from IBM‑owned delivery centers. Sub‑contracted sites, where used, are limited, transparent, and governed to the same standards—ensuring consistency, compliance, and performance across the global delivery network.

Active Financial Services Clients

Client Breakdown (Approximate, Active Managed Services Clients)

Issuers (Card‑Issuing Banks and Specialty Lenders)
30 – 40 active clients globally

Includes merchant acquirers and merchant‑services providers supported across onboarding and KYC, risk monitoring, chargebacks, settlements, merchant support, and operational back‑office functions.

Payments Processors / Networks / PSPs

Includes payment processors and payment service providers supported across transaction operations support, scheme compliance activities, disputes and chargebacks, fraud‑operations support, reconciliations, settlements, and finance operations.

Other Financial Services Clients

Additional Context
Across issuer, acquirer, and processor engagements, IBM supports tens of millions of customer accounts, millions of transactions annually, and large volumes of regulated operational workflows.

These engagements are typically multi‑tower and multi‑year, combining managed services with automation, AI‑enabled digital workers, and standardized governance and controls.

snippets.text:
Department of Treasury

$500M ($25M-30M annual) large-scale enterprise-wide digital transformation of MeF, the only means to electronically submit business and individual tax returns, involving 52 tax types and 1,790 separate forms with over 380M returns filed annually and over 3.5B returns since inception.

- Application modernization
- Process redesign

Modernization of legacy
core tax account systems
using Generative AI and
other AI Assets to convert
from legacy code to
modern event driven
architectures and
microservices in months
not years.

US / English

- Agentic AI-Driven Delivery Acceleration
- Operational System Redesign
- Straight-Through Digital Processing
- Core System Modernization
- AI-Enabled Fraud Prevention and Compliance

- Support spans three significantly diverse product environments, including 24/7 operations, over
10 lower environments, over 2.7M lines of code, and an average of 200 major form changes a
year
- MeF processes over 300M tax returns in January and February and, on a peak day (April 15), it
processes over 20M
- MeF must process hundreds to thousands of business rules against each return, including
integrating the response and interactive verification from up to three transaction interfaces with
other systems
- MeF uses an Application to Application (A2A) interface that allows transmitters and states to
submit – and retrieve – returns interactively with MeF, which processes 500 tax returns per
second. Each tax return has many child forms and attached dependencies, which all require
validation

- Piloting automation technology to streamline paper processing
- Repository for all taxpayer information filings
- Authenticates & Validates users & protects 30 million taxpayer IDs

channels. CEMIG’s goal is to achieve 90% of customer interaction over digital/self-service
channels
- CEMIG aims to be reference on energy distribution sector leading digital transformation
between peers

snippets.questionText: Demonstrate the expected improvement in SLAs/KPIs over time, from transition through steady state. Proposed commitments may be incorporated into contractual service level schedules.
snippets.text:
NA ~75% EMEA ~ 76%

Our WFM approach to SLA is fouces on capacity precision and execution discipline. We ensure accurate demand planning through robust forecasting methodologies and translate this into interval-level staffing plans that account for all shrinkage components.

SLA delivery is governed through continuous tracking of requirement vs. net staffing, schedule efficiency, adherence, and occupancy. Variances are analyzed at a granular level (interval/queue), enabling timely corrective actions in collaboration with Operations. This structured governance ensures that planned capacity is consistently realized on the floor, driving stable and predictable SLA outcomes.

60 to 120 Days

Depending on Client 30 - 60 seconds

20 Sec

ASA is managed as a real-time responsiveness metric, where WFM focuses on ensuring capacity availability at the exact moment of demand. Beyond overall staffing, emphasis is placed on interval coverage precision, particularly during demand peaks and intra-day fluctuations.

WFM actively manages Real-Time Adherence (RTA), break adherence, and intra-interval staffing gaps to minimize response delays. Intraday actions are driven by continuous monitoring of queue conditions, ensuring swift adjustments to staffing distribution and maintaining optimal queue flow. 

Through a combination of proactive planning, real-time control, and actionable insights, WFM enables improved queue responsiveness and consistent ASA perform

61 to 120 Days

NA ~3.78% EMEA ~3.30%

Abandonment Rate is a sensitivity metric driven by wait time volatility, and WFM directly influences it through coverage precision and intraday responsiveness.

Our approach focuses on interval-level demand–supply matching to prevent queue build-up at critical periods where abandonment risk is highest. Beyond baseline staffing, WFM actively manages coverage gaps (net staffing vs requirement) and ensures optimal distribution of resources across intervals and queues.

A key lever is Real-Time Adherence (RTA) and intraday control, where WFM continuously monitors queue thresholds and triggers immediate actions such as:

Reallocation of offline resources/Support Resources to queue
Break and activity re-optimization
Short-term capacity adjustments (OT)

We also track queue behavior indicators such as spikes in wait time, sudden volume surges, and interval-level SLA dips to proactively prevent abandonment rather than react to it.

Additionally, WFM provides granular insights on mismatch drivers (e.g., under-coverage intervals, high AHT impact, shrinkage deviations), enabling Operations to take targeted actions without delay

62 to 120 Days

Need baseline data

First Call Resolution (FCR) is primarily driven by agent capability and process effectiveness however, WFM plays a critical role by enabling the right conditions for quality resolution.

Our approach focuses on ensuring optimal staffing stability and workload balance, allowing agents sufficient time and focus to resolve customer queries effectively without undue pressure from queue congestion. By maintaining appropriate occupancy levels and minimizing overloading during peak intervals, 

WFM also provides data-driven insights on key drivers impacting FCR, such as repeat contact trends, AHT variations, and interval-level workload pressures. These insights enable Operations and Training teams to take targeted actions around coaching, knowledge gaps, and process improvements.

Additionally, WFM monitors ramp-up trends and productivity patterns, supporting better workforce readiness and consistency in service delivery.

90 to 180 days from stead state

>40% (Top Industry Quartile)

At IBM we focus heavily on soft‑skill development, including empathy, tone, rapport‑building, and service recovery. Agents learn how to create a positive emotional impact, especially in difficult situations, using consistent and customer‑friendly language.
Quality:

9 to 12 months due to frequency and methodology

Integrations rely on client‑provided APIs and standard data exchange protocols (JSON, XML, HTTPS). No proprietary API dependencies.

The proposed architecture supports a fully open integration model using REST, event‑based, and enterprise APIs.
All platform components — including AI, CCaaS, routing, and knowledge systems — integrate through standards‑based APIs offered by the client.
This ensures no data lock‑in, seamless interoperability, and the flexibility to replace or upgrade components without disrupting upstream or downstream systems.

Commit

Solution components only process data; they do not assume ownership. Data resides in client‑controlled systems of record.

All customer, operational, and interaction data remains within client‑owned systems of record, such as enterprise data lakes, CRM platforms, and backend account systems.
AI engines, CCaaS tools, and orchestration layers only process or reference this data; they do not become the authoritative repository.
Ownership, control, access rights, and retention policies remain solely under the client’s governance.

Commit

The architecture uses industry‑standard data structures and exchange formats (e.g., JSON, XML, CSV, Parquet).
All models, logs, analytical outputs, and customer interaction data are stored or exportable in open formats, ensuring portability and long‑term accessibility.

Because all systems integrate through client‑controlled APIs and data repositories, the client may extract:

Interaction transcripts
Training data
Routing logic
System configuration
Performance metrics
Audit and compliance logs
without dependency on proprietary tooling or additional licensing.
The engagement model ensures no incremental extraction cost at exit.

AI self‑service

Partial

Any new technology introduced within the engagement is deployed using industry‑standard architectures, minimizing incremental technical debt.
Where platform deployment or integration work generates new technical debt directly attributable to the solution, the provider commits to remediation at no added cost, ensuring long‑term maintainability and future‑state scalability.

Commit

Governance is aligned with industry standards and financial‑services regulatory expectations.
The AI operating model includes:

Bias measurement and mitigation

Audit trails
Human‑in‑the‑loop supervision
Governance is embedded across training, deployment, QA, and continuous optimization to ensure regulatory compliance.

Commit

Telephony and CCaaS remain client‑owned; only API connections and routing updates are required.
Self‑service digital workers, conversational AI, and agent‑assist capabilities integrate directly with:

Enterprise systems
Because routing and orchestration occur upstream, clients are not required to migrate or replace legacy telephony or servicing platforms.

The architecture connects to existing telephony and IVR systems through standards‑based integration.
Ownership of numbers, call flows, IVR designs, and telecom infrastructure remains 100% with the client, unless the client explicitly requests a transition.

End‑to‑end system architecture

AI + human coexistence workflows
Operational readiness criteria
Cutover and fallback scenarios
Data flow, security, and compliance overlays
This ensures transparency, predictability, and risk‑controlled activation.

Describe your approach to clients with explicit offshore restrictions in their current TMS contracts.

When offshore restrictions are in place due to regulatory, contractual, or operational requirements, IBM applies the following practices:
• Localization of processing: Where a client’s contract specifies that certain data or processes must remain onshore, IBM ensures that all processing, storage, and support activities remain strictly within the approved geography. • Restrictions on data transfer: If offshore restrictions prohibit the movement of personal or sensitive information outside the client‑approved jurisdiction, IBM ensures all systems, personnel, and workflows are architected to prevent any cross‑border transfer. • Client responsibility and oversight: The client retains primary responsibility for determining whether offshore activities align with their contractual, regulatory, and risk obligations. • Use of standardized contractual terms: Where offshore restrictions require explicit provisions or standardized language for any permitted cross‑border data movement, IBM incorporates approved model clauses or equivalents, pending client review and agreement.
IBM’s approach emphasizes strict adherence to onshore requirements, transparent communication, and a governance model that ensures that offshore restrictions defined in the client’s TMS contract are upheld without exception.

snippets.questionText: FDCPA / TCPA — describe compliance approach for collections operations in the United States.
snippets.text:
IBM maintains a comprehensive, controls‑driven compliance program to ensure that all US collections operations fully adhere to the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA), the Telephone Consumer Protection Act (TCPA), and other applicable federal and state consumer‑protection requirements. Our approach combines governance, operational controls, monitoring, and training to ensure compliant and consumer‑centric collection practices.
FDCPA compliance approach:
• Policy and process alignment: IBM collection workflows are designed to comply with FDCPA requirements regarding call frequency, timing, prohibited practices, disclosures, dispute handling, and consumer rights.
• Required disclosures: Agents follow scripts and procedures that include all mandatory FDCPA disclosures and validation notices.
• Prohibited practices controls: Systems and QA reviews enforce policies prohibiting harassment, misleading statements, unauthorized communication, or unfair practices.
• Dispute management: IBM adheres to strict procedures for documenting, pausing, and investigating disputes in accordance with FDCPA timing and validation requirements.
• Consumer rights protections: IBM ensures consumers can request verification, cease communication, or specify preferred communication channels in alignment with FDCPA guidelines.
TCPA compliance approach:
• Consent management: IBM only contacts consumers using automated dialing or prerecorded messages when the client confirms prior express consent or prior express written consent, as required.
• Contact method governance: Dialer systems are configured to block calls without verified consent indicators. Auto‑dialers, prerecorded messages, and SMS communications are used only in legally permissible scenarios.
• Call timing and opt‑out support: Contact attempts follow legally required time windows, opt‑out capabilities, and do‑not‑call suppression lists.
• Number validation: IBM uses tools to identify reassigned numbers and mobile vs. landline classification to minimize TCPA risk.
Oversight and monitoring:
• Quality monitoring and call reviews: IBM conducts routine QA reviews specifically focused on FDCPA/TCPA compliance, including call content, tone, disclosures, and adherence to consent and communication rules.
• Compliance governance: Dedicated legal, compliance, and risk teams oversee execution of regulatory mandates and provide ongoing guidance as regulations evolve.
• Complaint resolution: IBM maintains procedures to track, escalate, and resolve consumer complaints in alignment with FDCPA, TCPA, and client requirements.
Training and certification:
• Agents receive specialized FDCPA/TCPA training prior to handling collections accounts and recurrent refresher training thereafter.
• Training covers prohibited practices, mandatory disclosures, consent validation, communication rules, escalation procedures, and UDAAP risk mitigation.
• Supervisors receive advanced training to oversee compliance adherence and coach agents effectively.
Audit and documentation:
• Internal audits evaluate compliance with FDCPA, TCPA, and related consumer‑protection rules.
• IBM supports client‑driven audits and provides relevant certifications or summary audit reports as required under the contract or DPA.
Technology controls supporting compliance:
• Dialer controls enforce call restrictions, call‑frequency caps, consent status, and time‑of‑day rules.
• CRM and workflow systems document all consumer interactions, dispute entries, verifications, and opt‑outs.
• Secure data handling protects personal and financial information in alignment with federal and state laws.
In summary, IBM’s FDCPA/TCPA compliance model integrates strong governance, detailed process controls, advanced technology safeguards, continuous training, and rigorous monitoring to ensure that all collections activities in the United States meet regulatory standards and protect consumers.

snippets.questionText: CFPB — describe your compliance program and oversight model for collections, disputes, and customer service operations.
snippets.text:
IBM maintains a comprehensive compliance program designed to ensure that operations supporting collections, disputes, and customer service meet all applicable federal regulatory requirements, including those enforced by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB). Our compliance framework integrates policies, oversight structures, audit mechanisms, and operational controls that govern the fair, secure, and compliant handling of consumer interactions and financial data.
Compliance program aligned to CFPB expectations:
• IBM operates under a global set of privacy, security, and ethics directives that govern how we handle personal and financial data across all business units. These directives are aligned with US regulatory requirements, including CFPB standards for consumer protection, fair treatment, accuracy, and responsiveness.
• All client‑specific requirements related to collections, servicing, and dispute handling are implemented contractually and translated into operational procedures for delivery teams.
Oversight model for collections, disputes, and customer service operations:
• Compliance governance: Dedicated compliance, legal, and risk teams oversee adherence to regulatory and contractual obligations. These teams provide guidance, oversight, and periodic review of operational processes that directly affect consumers.
• Policies and controls: Controls are implemented to ensure accuracy in disclosures, proper handling of disputes, adherence to fair collections practices, secure treatment of financial data, and prevention of unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices (UDAAP).
• Training and competency: All personnel supporting consumer‑facing operations receive mandatory training on regulatory obligations, including CFPB requirements, UDAAP principles, complaint handling, dispute processing, and data security.
• Monitoring and quality assurance: IBM maintains robust quality monitoring programs to evaluate adherence to regulatory, client, and procedural requirements. Call handling, dispute documentation, consumer communications, and decision‑making processes are reviewed regularly.
• Complaint management: IBM follows structured processes for documenting, escalating, and resolving consumer complaints in accordance with CFPB expectations and client‑defined procedures.
• Reporting and transparency: IBM provides clients with regular operational reporting, compliance findings, and risk insights to support their broader regulatory responsibilities.
Audit and independent review:
• Internal audits: IBM conducts periodic internal audits of privacy, data handling, and operational processes relevant to collections, disputes, and service operations.
• Third‑party audits: Independent auditors review IBM’s practices annually. Findings are reflected in IBM’s annual disclosures and applicable certifications.
• Client audit support: Under the Data Processing Addendum (DPA), IBM provides clients with certifications, summary audit reports, and access to relevant documentation upon written request, supporting oversight of CFPB‑related obligations.
Contractual alignment:
• IBM implements all client‑mandated controls and obligations related to consumer protection, dispute resolution standards, and collections practices.
• Any additional requirements stemming from client policies or regulatory expectations are incorporated into the contract and applied to operations accordingly.
In summary, IBM maintains a rigorous compliance program supported by internal governance, audits, training, quality oversight, and contractual alignment to ensure that collections, disputes, and customer service operations meet CFPB expectations and protect consumers effectively.

snippets.questionText: Add any further regulatory requirements, jurisdictional constraints, or compliance considerations relevant to your proposed delivery model that are not covered above.
snippets.text:
IBM’s proposed delivery model incorporates all major regulatory and jurisdictional requirements associated with credit and commercial card fraud operations, disputes, collections, and customer servicing across the United States, United Kingdom, and other relevant locations. Beyond the regulations already addressed (GDPR, CCPA/US state privacy laws, PCI DSS, FDCPA, TCPA, FCA, CFPB, and general outsourcing/offshoring regulatory requirements), the following additional considerations are relevant to ensure full compliance:
• Payment network rules:
IBM adheres to card scheme regulations and operating rules issued by Visa, Mastercard, American Express, and Discover, including dispute processing standards, fraud alert expectations, chargeback timelines, documentation requirements, and secure handling of PAN‑related data.
• Record retention and e‑discovery:
Data retention policies must comply with jurisdiction‑specific financial regulations and contract terms, including requirements for retaining dispute records, correspondence, fraud investigations, and consumer communications for mandated periods.
• Bank‑specific confidentiality and outsourcing guidelines:
For work supporting regulated financial institutions, IBM aligns its controls with relevant banking regulations (e.g., OCC, FFIEC, EBA, PRA guidance on outsourcing and operational resilience). This includes requirements for governance, monitoring, subcontractor management, and resilience planning.
• Operational resilience and business continuity:
Where applicable, IBM ensures alignment with:
– UK FCA/PRA operational resilience rules
– US FFIEC business continuity guidance
– Client‑specific resilience frameworks
This includes impact tolerances, scenario testing, and continuity strategies for fraud, dispute, and servicing operations.
• Data localization and cross‑border rules (non‑EU/UK/US):
Depending on the final delivery locations, IBM considers requirements such as:
– India’s DPDP Act for personal data handling
– Philippines DPA for BPO operations
– Any country‑specific constraints related to employee monitoring, call recording, or data transfer
IBM ensures compliance with local labor, privacy, and telecommunications rules that impact service delivery.
• Call recording and monitoring regulations:
Jurisdictions have varying rules regarding call monitoring, consent, retention, and consumer disclosure (e.g., two‑party consent states in the US, UK call‑recording rules under PECR). IBM aligns call‑recording practices with applicable law and ensures proper disclosures.
• Employment and labor law compliance for BPO locations:
IBM ensures compliance with local employment, labor, and workforce‑protection requirements—including Working Time Regulations (UK), US state labor laws, and offshore location rules—to maintain compliant staffing models.
• Information security certifications and industry standards:
In addition to PCI DSS, IBM maintains alignment with ISO 27001, NIST 800‑53, and other industry‑standard frameworks that provide assurance of strong controls across global delivery sites.
If no additional regulatory, contractual, or jurisdictional constraints apply beyond those listed above and previously provided, IBM ensures that its delivery model remains adaptable and fully compliant with any client‑specific or newly introduced regulatory requirements.
In summary, IBM’s regulatory and compliance framework is comprehensive, adaptable across jurisdictions, and designed to meet all applicable laws, card‑network rules, financial‑sector regulations, and client‑specific standards associated with global fraud, dispute, collections, and servicing operations.

snippets.questionText: GDPR — confirm DPA agreements, data processor obligations, cross-border transfer mechanisms, and breach notification procedures (including SLA to notify FIS).
snippets.text:
IBM acts as a Data Processor under GDPR and processes personal data solely on the documented instructions of the client, unless legally required otherwise. The scope of processing, permitted uses, and data‑handling obligations are defined and governed through the IBM Data Processing Addendum (DPA), the accompanying DPA Exhibit, and the client’s configuration and use of the contracted services.
Data Processor obligations:
• IBM processes personal data only as instructed by the client.
• IBM implements appropriate technical and organizational measures to protect personal data, including access control, encryption, monitoring, and security governance aligned with GDPR Article 28 requirements.
• IBM retains or deletes personal data only in accordance with the client’s instructions or as required by law. IBM does not independently determine what data is necessary for the client’s purposes or delete data without explicit instruction if the client cannot perform the deletion themselves.
• IBM ensures personnel with access to personal data are bound by confidentiality and are trained on GDPR obligations.
• IBM supports the client with GDPR compliance needs, including supporting DPIAs, responding to data subject rights requests (when applicable), and facilitating audits required under the DPA.
Cross‑border transfer mechanisms:
Where international transfers are required and approved by the client, IBM uses GDPR‑compliant transfer mechanisms. These include:
• EU Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) for international data transfers.
• Supplementary measures where required to ensure appropriate levels of protection.
• Regional hosting options to avoid cross‑border transfers when clients elect or require in‑region processing.
No cross‑border transfers are performed unless explicitly permitted under the client’s instructions and the applicable DPA.
Breach notification procedures:
IBM maintains a formal incident detection, escalation, and response process.
• In the event of a confirmed personal data breach affecting the client’s personal data, IBM notifies the client without undue delay, as required under GDPR Article 33.
• IBM can align with client‑specific notification SLAs, including any FIS‑mandated timeframes, and typically provides initial notification within the contracted SLA window.
• Notifications include available details about the nature of the breach, data affected, mitigation steps taken, and ongoing remediation actions.
DPA agreements:
• All processing of personal data is governed by the IBM DPA, which incorporates GDPR requirements and defines responsibilities between IBM (as Processor) and the client (as Controller).
• The DPA remains a mandatory component of the contractual relationship for any service involving personal data.
This framework ensures IBM meets GDPR processor obligations, maintains secure and compliant data handling, and provides timely breach notifications consistent with FIS requirements.

snippets.questionText: Describe security controls for remote/WFH agents handling cardholder data (screen capture, device management, network security, print restrictions).
snippets.text:
IBM enforces strict security controls for remote and Work‑From‑Home (WFH) agents who handle cardholder data. These controls are aligned with PCI DSS requirements and IBM’s internal security standards to ensure that sensitive data remains protected at all times.
Screen capture controls:
• IBM disables or restricts screen capture, screen recording, and local screen‑sharing capabilities on managed devices used by remote agents.
• DLP (Data Loss Prevention) tools monitor and block attempts to capture or transmit screen content containing sensitive information.
• Clipboard and copy/paste functions may be restricted during handling of cardholder data, based on client requirements.
Device management:
• Remote agents use IBM‑managed and secured devices that enforce endpoint protection, full‑disk encryption, anti‑malware, and vulnerability management.
• Devices are configured to require multi‑factor authentication and strong password controls.
• Only approved, hardened devices can access client environments. Personal devices are not permitted for cardholder data workflows.
• IBM’s device management platform ensures continuous monitoring, policy enforcement, and remote wipe capability in the event of loss or compromise.
Network security:
• All remote access to IBM or FIS systems requires a secure, encrypted channel—typically via VPN or zero‑trust network access solutions.
• Network access controls prevent connection from untrusted or non‑compliant networks.
• Firewalls, intrusion detection/prevention, and secure DNS filtering help ensure remote agents access systems from a secure environment.
• Public Wi‑Fi usage is restricted unless combined with client‑approved secure tunneling protocols.
Print restrictions:
• Printing of cardholder data is prohibited for remote/WFH agents unless explicitly authorized by the client and protected via secure printing methods.
• Print functions on remote agent devices are disabled by policy to prevent unauthorized printing.
• If approved, secure printers require badge release, logging, and immediate collection protocols.
Additional safeguards:
• Workspace requirements ensure that remote agents operate in a private, controlled environment to prevent shoulder surfing or unauthorized observation.
• Logging and monitoring tools track remote session activity, access attempts, and policy violations in real time.
This combined set of technical, administrative, and procedural controls ensures that remote/WFH agents handling cardholder data operate within a secure, compliant environment that meets or exceeds PCI DSS and FIS requirements.

snippets.questionText: US cardholder data — confirm CCPA compliance and your approach to state-level privacy law variation.
snippets.text:
IBM maintains comprehensive global data privacy policies and standards governing the collection, use, storage, retention, disclosure, and protection of personal information, including US cardholder data. These standards are designed to ensure compliance with federal requirements as well as state‑level laws such as the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) and the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA).
CCPA/CPRA compliance:
• IBM acts as a “Service Provider” when processing US cardholder data on behalf of clients and processes data only for permissible business purposes defined in the contract.
• IBM does not use client data for cross‑context behavioral advertising or any purpose outside the agreed scope.
• IBM supports CCPA/CPRA requirements related to data minimization, purpose limitation, secure handling, and consumer rights fulfillment. Any consumer rights requests received by IBM are escalated to the client (as the Business) in accordance with contractual obligations.
• IBM conducts regular internal audits and third‑party reviews to validate adherence to privacy standards and state‑level compliance requirements.
Approach to state-level privacy law variation:
• IBM’s global privacy program is designed to comply with a broad spectrum of evolving state privacy laws, including those enacted in states such as Virginia, Colorado, Connecticut, Utah, and others.
• IBM applies a unified privacy framework that aligns with the strictest common requirements (e.g., CCPA/CPRA), enabling consistent compliance across multiple US jurisdictions.
• Where state laws introduce specific obligations—such as enhanced opt‑out rights, special rules for sensitive personal information, or unique contractual terms—IBM incorporates these into service agreements, internal controls, and operational procedures.
• IBM continuously monitors state‑level legislative developments and updates policies and controls to ensure ongoing compliance as new privacy requirements are introduced.
In summary, IBM confirms compliance with CCPA and state‑level privacy requirements through its global privacy policies, disciplined governance structure, and auditing practices. IBM’s approach ensures that US cardholder data is processed in accordance with applicable federal and state privacy laws and with consistent, high‑integrity controls across jurisdictions.

snippets.questionText: Confirm FIS retains full ownership and portability of all data at all times. Describe the mechanism for full data extraction upon contract exit.
snippets.text:
IBM confirms that FIS retains full ownership, control, and portability of all data at all times. IBM processes FIS data solely on the documented instructions of FIS and does not claim any rights, title, or interest in the data processed on FIS’s behalf. IBM’s role remains that of a data processor and service provider, acting strictly under the terms defined by the contract and Data Processing Addendum (DPA).
Ownership and portability:
• FIS retains full ownership of all data, including cardholder data, operational data, logs, outputs, and derived data produced as part of the contracted services.
• IBM does not use FIS data for any purpose other than providing the contracted services and does not restrict or limit FIS’s right to retrieve, export, or transfer its data at any time.
• Data portability remains fully under FIS’s control, and IBM acts only on FIS’s instructions regarding retention, extraction, or deletion.
Mechanism for full data extraction upon contract exit:
• Upon contract termination or expiration, IBM enables FIS to extract all data in a commonly used, machine‑readable format, consistent with the provisions outlined in the DPA and the service agreement.
• IBM provides reasonable assistance, including access to tools, export utilities, or secure file transfer processes, to facilitate complete extraction of all FIS data stored or processed within the service environment.
• The timing, method, and format of data extraction are agreed upon in the exit plan or service‑specific contractual terms to ensure an orderly, secure transition.
• After FIS has confirmed successful receipt and validation of all exported data, IBM will securely delete any remaining FIS data from its systems in accordance with FIS’s instructions and applicable regulatory and contractual requirements, providing deletion certification where required.
• All extraction and deletion activities are performed using secure transfer and handling methods to maintain data integrity, confidentiality, and regulatory compliance.
This approach ensures that FIS maintains complete ownership and portability of its data throughout the engagement and receives full support for secure and comprehensive data extraction at contract exit.

snippets.questionText: Confirm logical and/or physical segregation of cardholder data across different FIS clients.
snippets.text:
IBM confirms that cardholder data for each FIS client is logically and, where applicable, physically segregated to ensure full isolation, confidentiality, and compliance with PCI DSS and contractual requirements. Segregation is a core control within IBM’s security architecture and is enforced through multiple layers of technical and administrative safeguards.
IBM’s approach to data segregation includes the following:
• Logical segregation:
IBM implements strict logical separation between client environments using access control policies, network segmentation, dedicated tenant identifiers, and role‑based access management. This ensures that cardholder data belonging to one FIS client cannot be accessed, viewed, or processed by another client or any unauthorized party.
• Physical segregation (as required):
For clients whose contracts or regulatory requirements mandate dedicated infrastructure or isolated computing environments, IBM can deploy physically segregated systems to ensure no shared hardware, storage, or network components are used across clients.
• PCI DSS–aligned controls:
IBM applies PCI DSS requirements related to multi‑factor authentication, least‑privilege access, encryption of cardholder data in transit and at rest, and continuous monitoring. These controls support secure separation between client datasets and prevent unauthorized cross‑client access.
• Multi‑tenancy safeguards:
In environments that support multiple clients, IBM enforces strong tenancy boundaries that prevent data mingling. Segregation controls are validated through regular audits, vulnerability assessments, and access reviews.
• Contractual adherence:
IBM’s segregation approach is tailored to the specific requirements outlined in FIS contracts. If FIS mandates enhanced isolation or dedicated environments for certain clients or programs, IBM aligns the solution accordingly.
In summary, IBM ensures that cardholder data from each FIS client remains fully isolated through robust logical controls and, where required, physical separation. These safeguards prevent any cross‑client data exposure and ensure compliance with PCI DSS and FIS security expectations.
